method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, near the Solomon Islands.[2][3] It lies about 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2] The volcano is situated in the Pacific Ring of Fire at a convergent plate boundary where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[1][2] Kavachi rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying only about 20 m below sea level.[2] Bathymetric observations describe the main summit as an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring about 120 m by 75 m, with a rim at an average depth of 24 m.[5] The volcano’s flanks descend with slopes of about 18° to depths greater than 1,000 m.[5] A secondary summit, described as a southwest extension, lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and rises to a depth of about 260 m below sea level.[5] Because of its shallow summit, eruptions can discolor the surrounding water and produce plumes visible from aircraft and satellites.[2] Kavachi has also periodically built short-lived islands above the sea surface during eruptive phases.[1][3][4]

==Marine life==
During quiescent phases, Kavachi’s crater has been found to contain marine life despite warm, acidic, mineral-rich hydrothermal waters.[2][4] A 2015 expedition documented an ecosystem that included gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities and sharks within the crater.[4][5] The sharks recorded included scalloped hammerheads and silky sharks, which were filmed swimming in the crater and reacting to baited cameras.[4] Measurements reported from the crater described water temperatures up to about 13 °C above the surrounding sea, reaching approximately 42 °C, and a pH of around 6.1 in waters affected by carbon dioxide- and sulfur-rich volcanic gases.[4] The suspended-particle plume has been interpreted as a habitat for adapted microbial populations, including bacteria of the genus Sulfurimonas capable of chemosynthesis using sulfur and carbon dioxide.[4] The presence of large predators in these conditions has made Kavachi a natural laboratory for studying extreme marine ecosystems and tolerance to heat and acidity.[1][5] It remains uncertain whether sharks live in the crater continuously or enter during calmer periods, with proposed explanations including behavioural avoidance of eruptions, sensitivity to chemical or seismic warning signs, and physiological tolerance of harsh water conditions.[1][5] Observations from Kavachi have also been discussed as relevant to marine resilience under warming oceans and changing ocean chemistry.[1][5]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi has produced frequent shallow submarine eruptions since its first recorded eruption in 1939, with activity often reaching the sea surface and building temporary islands.[1] At least nine islands have been reported to form during eruptive episodes, but they were too small and poorly consolidated to withstand wave erosion and typically disappeared within months.[1] Historical accounts from late 1951 describe frequent small tsunamis associated with activity at the volcano.[1]

After about nine years of dormancy, observers aboard the CSIRO research vessel *Franklin* documented renewed island-building activity on 14 May 2000.[2] During that episode, explosions threw ash and incandescent lava blocks as high as 70 m above sea level, while sulphurous steam plumes rose to about 500 m.[2] Explosive eruptions were reported daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and by January 2002 debris, gas bubbles, vigorous surface disturbance, and loud noises were again observed.[2]

Another eruptive cycle in 2003 produced a temporary island about 15 m high, but by mid-November the summit had been reduced to roughly 32 m below sea level.[2] Activity was again reported in March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2] A rare lull in January 2015 allowed researchers to approach and survey the active crater, a task normally limited by Kavachi’s explosive behavior.[3] Subsequent work during that lull documented ongoing hydrothermal discharge and gas flux at the summit, indicating that volcanic and hydrothermal processes continued even outside major eruptive episodes.[3][5]
